title: Synthetic Identity Bust-Out: 90-Day Nurture to Sudden $8K-$15K Depletion
narrative: A synthetic identity is aged through 12-18 small legitimate purchases ($15-$75) over 90-120 days, establishing clean payment history across 2-3 merchants with matching MCC codes (5411 grocery, 5912 pharmacy). The account maintains static P_emaildomain (free provider like yahoo.com) and consistent addr1 but shows no organic behavior variation. On day 91-95, the pattern shifts violently: 6-9 transactions within 48 hours totaling $8,000-$15,000, all CNP with ProductCD='W' (internet purchases), hitting high-risk MCCs (5732 electronics, 5944 jewelry). Card velocity metrics spike from baseline 0.4 txn/week to 4+ txn/day. The bust-out targets credit limits established during the nurture phase, often cleared within 72 hours before first payment due date.
indicators: card1_txn_count_7day jumps from 0-1 baseline to 6-9 within 48-hour window after 90+ days dormancy
TransactionAmt mean shifts from $15-$75 range to $800-$2,500 per transaction with no gradual escalation
All bust-out transactions share ProductCD='W' and occur during business hours (txn_hour 9-17) versus prior varied timing
P_emaildomain remains unchanged free provider but R_emaildomain switches to high-risk temporary domains
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 3.5 standard deviations above 90-day account baseline within 48-hour period
Merchant category switches from low-risk MCCs (5411, 5912) to high-risk (5732, 5944, 5999) exclusively
Total exposure reaches 85-100% of estimated credit limit within 72 hours of first elevated transaction
summary: Account shows 90-120 days of small, consistent purchases at grocery or pharmacy merchants, then suddenly executes 6-9 large online transactions ($8K-$15K total) within 48 hours at electronics or jewelry retailers. Transaction velocity spikes from less than one per week to over four per day, with individual amounts jumping from $15-$75 to $800-$2,500.
reasoning: Fraudsters build synthetic identities with months of normal activity to establish credit limits and trust, then execute a coordinated bust-out to max out available credit before disappearing. The abrupt shift from patient nurturing behavior to aggressive spending at high-value merchants signals the account was never a real customer, just a vehicle for final exploitation.
indicator_explanations: Card suddenly springs to life with burst activity after three months of silence
Purchase amounts skyrocket from modest to massive with no warm-up period
All high-value hits use identical product code during standard business hours only
Recipient email flips to sketchy throwaway domains while sender email stays put
Spending shoots far beyond what this account has ever done historically
Card abruptly switches from grocery stores to electronics and cash-equivalent merchants
Account gets maxed out to near credit limit within three days
